narrative: As a Black male applicant, your law school application was evaluated using a predictive model that assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, based on academic and background factors from past students. The model estimated a 52% probability that you would fail the bar exam, which crossed the threshold for rejection, as the school prioritizes admitting those predicted to succeed strongly. This decision hinged on just a few key aspects of your profile, with your LSAT score, Year 3 school decile ranking, and undergraduate GPA standing out as the most influential. Your LSAT score of 42, notably higher than the typical 36.7 average, worked strongly in your favor, pushing the prediction toward expecting you to pass by demonstrating solid analytical skills. However, your Year 3 decile ranking of 2—well below the average of 5.53—had the opposite effect, signaling to the model a significant dip in performance that raised concerns about sustaining momentum through law school and the bar. Your undergraduate GPA of 2.4, lower than the 3.21 average, also contributed toward the failure prediction, though its influence was milder compared to your Year 3 grades. Other elements, like your middle family income bracket and full-time student status (common among 93% of applicants), played smaller roles. In contrast to typical students who pass, you outperformed on LSAT but trailed in later academic deciles and undergrad grades, tipping the balance. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a Black man pursuing law—many with similar backgrounds overcome these metrics through targeted growth. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT if possible to build on your strength there, or focus on boosting your GPA through post-bac courses or explaining your Year 3 context in a revised personal statement. Strengthening these areas could shift future predictions in your favor for other schools. Keep pushing; your LSAT shows you're capable of the rigor ahead.
